data.title: Invitation Homes And American Homes 4 Rent Just Got Interesting
data.description:
Single-Family Rental (SFR) REITs dropped materially on news that Trump is considering banning large institutional investors from buying single-family homes. It is the latest leg down (drop on right side of chart) in what has already been a down year.

For years we have not had much interest in the single-family rental REITs because they traded at premium valuations, and we considered them overvalued. However, with the latest drop, they have finally entered value territory.

With newly interesting market prices, we will be adding Invitation Homes (INVH) and American Homes 4 Rent (AMH) to our active coverage.

Let us begin with a discussion of industry level fundamentals and follow with the valuation and outlook of the individual REITs.

A successful sector facing a current headwind

The SFR REITs have performed very well on a fundamental level, each growing AFFO/share materially and consistently.

A key contributor to their success has been their ability to leverage scale to improve both margins and customer satisfaction.

Single-Family Rental, aside from the big REITs, is highly fragmented, consisting primarily of small businesses and individuals that own a handful of homes available for rent.

With a small number of homes, it is difficult for these smaller entities to have sufficient in-house expertise to deal with the variety of home repairs that are required over time. Some of the work has to be contracted out which hurts margins, and it is then done whenever the outside source has the time which hurts customer satisfaction.

The SFR REITs have a different level of scale. INVH and AMH own over 85,000 and 61,000 homes, respectively. Each services thousands more homes through JV ownership and/or 3rd party management. They concentrate their holdings such that they will have a few thousand homes in individual submarkets.

With that level of scale, it becomes efficient to have highly experienced property managers and mechanics in close proximity to the homes. This allows them to service customer requests faster and more cost effectively. The proximity also means they need fewer personnel per home.

AMH and INVH achieved 3Q25 rental operating margins of 55% and 56%, respectively.

Historically, the sector has achieved strong rental rate growth and consistently high occupancy. When the cost of houses shot up circa 2021, renting became more economical relative to owning.

High home prices were exacerbated by mortgage rates shooting up to over 7% which blockaded an increasing portion of the population out of home ownership. It proved to be a boon for rental demand.

This allowed both apartments and SFR REITs to increase rent significantly year after year while maintaining high occupancy.

In response to the prosperous economics of rentals, developers flooded the market with supply. Development of SFR boomed in 2022 with an extraordinary volume of deliveries starting in 2023.

While the development wave has subsided with regard to new starts, those already in the pipeline are still being delivered at an above average pace.

This wave of supply meant incumbent properties had to compete with new builds that often offer lease-up incentives. It has been a headwind for rental rates across the entire sector. Submarkets with more new supply felt greater pressure on rents with many submarkets dipping to slightly negative rent growth in recent periods.

Yardi Matrix breaks down the rent and occupancy changes by submarket:

The supply wave took a bite out of same-store NOI growth.

Invitation Homes’ same-store NOI growth dropped to about 1% from its former average of about 4%. This is largely due to them being concentrated in Phoenix, Atlanta and Florida markets, which each received a huge wave of supply.

American Homes 4 Rent was less impacted, largely keeping its ~4% organic same-store NOI growth rate.

AMH is a bit more spread out and owns properties in submarkets that were less supply heavy.

In the longer run it is unclear to me which property markets are better. While markets like Phoenix and Atlanta are hurting from the supply wave, they are excellent demand markets with strong job and population growth so I suspect they will bounce back nicely as supply subsides.

That is our overview of the SFR industry. It is high margin and has generally had strong organic growth but it is currently facing a headwind from the supply wave of 2022-2023 with the tail end of properties constructed at that time still being delivered.

As forward supply looks much lower while demand remains healthy, I suspect SFR will return to a healthy organic growth rate after a lull in 2025 and early 2026. There is still significant room for higher rental rates due to the abnormally large spread in affordability between renting and owning.

It is a sector with generally strong fundamentals which is why it has typically traded at high AFFO multiples. With the downtrend in pricing and the recent drop on the talk of banning institutional buying of homes, the AFFO multiples have gotten much cheaper.

Valuation

Below we charted AFFO in orange bars against market price. Note how AMH price to AFFO peaked in late 2021. Since 2021, however, market price has dropped materially while AFFO has continued to rise.

INVH has the same pattern except to an even more dramatic degree.

With higher AFFO and lower market price, they are now trading at the following multiples:

  • INVH – 16.0X AFFO
  • AMH – 17.8X AFFO

That puts them in-line with the REIT index, which is unusual for a sector that has consistently traded at a premium.

We at 2nd Market Capital like to look at valuation from a variety of angles. Using data from the 10-Ks of each company we estimated their enterprise values per square foot and per home.

INVH and AMH are trading at $288K and $302K per home, respectively. INVH and AMH have average home sizes of 1,880 and 1,990 square feet.

Most people have looked at Zillow and other home buying sites so this is a metric that can easily be compared to real-world pricing. The EV/home looks to be slightly below market value of the houses.

Note that this is only looking at the wholly owned portion of their portfolios. INVH and AMH also have partial interest in another 7,622 and 3,376 houses. INVH also does 3rd party management for another 17,6278 homes.

Overall, both companies are trading at sizable discounts to net asset value:

  • INVH- 68.7% of NAV
  • AMH – 75.7% of NAV

Fundamental Outlook

Near term AFFO growth looks a bit subdued, largely as a result of the supply wave. Forward consensus estimates for each company are shown below.

I think the market in pricing these companies down has over-indexed on the near term.

Slow AFFO/share growth during a heavy headwind looks quite healthy to me. These are good companies that operate well and have consistently grown on a per share basis.

In my opinion, they are trading below fundamental value and are opportunistic. INVH would be my pick in the sector as it is both trading at a cheaper multiple and the larger of the 2 companies. I think it is being overly punished for weakness in Phoenix and Atlanta. While these submarkets are temporarily weak due to supply, they are excellent markets longer-term.

There are a lot of strong opportunities in REITs right now with the sector being broadly cheap, so we are not investing heavily in SFR as there are just too many things we want to buy. That said, I think it would be entirely reasonable to buy INVH at current valuation. It is a good growth company trading at a reasonable price.

Risk and opportunity related to a potential ban of institutional buying of homes

The current administration’s talk about banning institutional buying of homes is in its early stages. Until more details are released it is quite difficult to estimate the chance of it happening or the impact.

However, markets are forward looking, and it behooves us as investors to consider the possibilities.

So far, the market has priced it in as unequivocally negative. Prima facie, it sounds like a negative to have a company banned from buying its primary asset class.

However, anything that messes with supply can be quite beneficial to properties already in place.

As seen by the lower same-store NOI during the current supply wave, new supply is one of the largest risks to real estate. If institutions are banned in some way from buying SFR, the existing properties of INVH and AMH would not have to compete with as much new supply.

There would likely be less volume produced and what did filter into the SFR market would largely just be mom-and-pop operators which cannot effectively compete on margins or customer satisfaction.

Thus, a ban of some sort could have negative implications for external growth but also positive implications for organic growth. There is not yet enough information to estimate which impact would be larger. It is also unclear at this point if anything will actually happen with regard to the proposed ban.

Key pieces of information to watch for:

  • How all-inclusive would the ban be?
  • Can institutions still buy homes directly from homebuilders?
  • Can institutions buy homes out of foreclosure?
  • Can institutions buy homes on the secondary market?

Each outcome has different implications so I will be watching news on this matter as well as the market prices of the SFR REITs. My hunch is that the market’s initial reaction to news may not factor in the subtle implications to supply/demand.

data.title: ImmunityBio: Making Strides, But Beware The Hype (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Topline Summary and Update

Market and analyst sentiment on ImmunityBio, Inc. (IBRX) has waxed and waned since I have been watching the company starting back in late 2023, prior to the approval of their first agent in bladder cancer. We're now finally getting clearer pictures of the market potential of this agent, as well as a wave of news related to their developmental pipeline, which now includes cellular therapies trying to build on engineered programs that we've had for a few years.

Today, let's focus on the results that have driven the surge in market value of IBRX stock from a low of around $2 to well over $5 per share at the time of writing, as well as why I think this rally is likely running a bit hot for now.

Pipeline Updates

Anktiva

The primary project continuing to be developed by IBRX is Anktiva, a "superagonist" of interleukin-15 designed to stimulate the production and activity of different immune cell populations in the context of cancer treatment. It is approved for the treatment of non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer (NMIBC) that has stopped responding to BCG, a weakened form of tuberculosis that is the current frontline standard of care after surgery in patients with early bladder cancer. Of note, this approval covers carcinoma in situ, with or without papillary histology, providing some important guardrails on the approved label at this time in the United States.

The biggest story for IBRX in 2025 related to challenges to get the label expanded to include patients who do not have carcinoma in situ, with the FDA issuing a refusal to file notice for the supplemental application. IBRX refuted that notice, stating that the FDA had provided "unambiguous guidance" earlier in the year that led them to submit the application. This disagreement has yet to be resolved, although IBRX has announced that the Saudi FDA has approved Anktiva for the wider label within their jurisdiction. Anktiva is also approved in Europe for carcinoma in situ with or without papillary histology.

For 2026, the next major clinical catalyst being showcased by IBRX is a potential readout of QUILT-2.005, evaluating Anktiva in patients with BCG-naive NMIBC, as enrollment has exceeded the expected pace and is on track to be complete by the next quarter. IBRX expects to use the findings from this study (primary endpoints being complete response rate and duration of response). We've previously seen results from the phase 1 portion of this trial, suggesting that adding Anktiva to BCG could improve the complete response rate at any time by 24% (85% vs. 61% for the experimental and BCG-only arms, respectively).

Those findings were encouraging, although limited in scope since the study only included 43 patients. However, the phase 1 readout gives a very interesting signal about the phase 2 trial, and I would view the readout later this year with optimism.

Circling back on ex-U.S. developments, the Saudi FDA was the first to approve Anktiva in the setting of non-small cell lung cancer. This was based on findings from a pair of studies in the metastatic setting, QUILT-3.055 and QUILT-2.023, which I covered in some detail in my last article.

Couple that with a recently announced readout showing strong recovery of lymphocytes, and the NSCLC continues to look promising to me. These developments in lung cancer were, in fact, the impetus for shifting my sentiment from relatively negative to tentatively positive at that time. A confirmatory phase 3 trial combining Anktiva with tislelizumab (an anti-PD-1 antibody) and docetaxel in previously treated NSCLC is ongoing.

Cell Therapy Programs

Anktiva also plays into the nascent pipeline of cell therapies being developed by IBRX. These include engineered NK cells targeting CD19 or PD-L1, as memory cytokine-enriched NK cells (M-ceNK cells). The CD19 program was the subject of the most recent news release on this front. IBRX announced that CD19-directed NK cells could induce durable remission in the setting of Waldenström macroglobulinemia, a form of non-Hodgkin lymphoma.

The results were taken from patients enrolled in the ongoing QUILT-106 study, which continues to enroll patients to receive treatment with these NK cells, and a follow-up study is planned to combine Anktiva with these cells.

Financial Overview

At the end of Q3 2025, IBRX held $60.2 million in cash and equivalents, as well as $197.6 million in marketable securities. Notable debts include $500.8 million in related-part convertible notes, as well as $316.1 million in revenue interest.

Product revenue was $31.8 million for the quarter, up from $5.9 million in Q3 2024. In a recent announcement, IBRX guided a preliminary net product revenue for 2025 of $113 million.

Operating expenses reached $87.7 million, comparable to the year prior, and after interest and other accounting items, the net loss for the quarter was $67.3 million. For the first 9 months of 2025, IBRX used $234.6 million in cash for operating activities. This implies a cash runway of approximately 3 to 4 quarters, assuming no other interventions.

It is worth noting that IBRX conducted various equity raises in 2025, bringing in almost $350 million. They have continued to prove resilient in terms of financing through Nant Capital, a private investment vehicle owned by the company's CMO.

Strengths and Risks

Strength - Strong chance to read out a definitive study in 2026

IBRX has a real shot to pursue its second major approval within the next few years. The thesis that they can achieve a dramatic revenue ramp is being unfurled before our eyes, and expanding labels and carving out new niches remain the key strategies that might push this to a long-term win. IBRX looks to be successfully executing both on the front of exploiting the current NMIBC label to its fullest extent in conjunction with finding where else the drug fits.

And if they're able to succeed on any of their cell therapy fronts, that would further solidify the growing juggernaut that the market is currently anticipating.

Risk - Debt load remains high

With a debt load sitting above $800 million, IBRX stands alone among the early commercial biotechs in terms of their outlays. This would appear to be an insurmountable climb, but it is worth keeping in mind that the debt is mostly held by entities controlled by the CMO Patrick Soon-Shiong. Still, servicing this debt is a drag on the road to profitability in the long term, and it is something that you should keep your eyes wide open on if considering an investment.

Risk - Despite sales growth, profitability remains far on the horizon

All talk aside about the growth of sales and the company's recent showcase of the eye-popping "700% growth in net product revenue," it is worth keeping in perspective that this growth is due mainly to being in the first full year of sales. And those net revenues of approximately $113 million are nothing to sneeze at, but they pale in comparison to other companies with one successful drug, such as Exelixis. And they pale in comparison with operating expenses, covering just over 1 quarter of the company's current outlay. The latter company has succeeded in building a major franchise brick by brick, approval by approval, not only by fully exploiting their first main opportunity.

It is important to keep in perspective the fact that setbacks like those we saw with the FDA in 2025 are not going to be one-off challenges. Bad news can and will visit IBRX as they continue to develop Anktiva further, and their cell therapy plays are very much in the proving stages and cannot yet be counted on to drive the pipeline further.

Bottom Line Summary

IBRX continues to have the legs of a stock with a large, devoted following, which has provided longevity far exceeding what I would view as a reasonable expectation. The rub is that, unlike my first negative sentiments of this stock, IBRX is now executing, and we're seeing signs that they have what it takes to make Anktiva a really successful product. When will that turn the company into a profitable venture? That's anyone's best guess, and a lot of people are betting right now that it's closer than you think.

If you're in that camp, it's hard to argue right now that there is much reason for pessimism with respect to IBRX. They have generated a lot of good news, new inroads, new approvals (outside the United States), and remain poised to announce potentially groundbreaking results in new indications this year.

Does that justify the current 46 price-to-sales ratio for the company, assuming the $113 million figure cited by the company is accurate? Time will tell, and IBRX likely has shown an ability to fund their ventures throughout. But for the near term, I think this hype is likely to deflate somewhat, and while the long-term story for IBRX remains optimistic (assuming they continue to execute), I expect we'll see a more favorable entry point before reaching those lofty goals being predicted by devoted stockholders.

As such, after substantial gains from my last Buy sentiment, I am revising to a Hold for now, given the uncertainty in terms of execution and what I suspect is a strong likelihood for a correction as market attention and enthusiasm wane.

data.title: Neste: 2025's Tremendous Returns Are The Start Of Recovery
data.description:
It's not fair or accurate to say that Neste has recovered or beaten my long-term estimates since I started writing about the company. Neste (NTOIF) has still been a very volatile company with plenty of ups and downs that, for many investors, including me, make it dependent on the positioning and timing, whether you're profitable or not. But I did buy shares and expand my position in 2025. And the return for doing that, if we look at the company's return for today, is massive. Massive is not a word I use lightly - I reserve it for triple-digit returns and similar "beats" that we see rarely.

We see it now, and we see it with Neste.

For the full story on Neste, I'm obviously recommending that you check out the entirety of my article series on the company, which is about 2-3 years old at the time of writing this article. I believe that the takeaway for this article, however, should be that undervalued stocks tend to recover at some point - and patience is key. Patience and not investing in companies that are arguably overvalued. Unfortunately, one of the things I did initially when I started investing in Neste was go in a bit "too early". It's something that is a tendency of mine, both on the "going in" and on the "going out".

One of the main questions for this article is whether I'll be selling my stake, divesting, and taking profits. For the time being, I don't believe that I will. The danger of the overvalued market is there, but I believe that Neste's (comparatively small, by the way) recovery marks not a peak in valuation, but merely the start to a recovery trend.

Of course, if you're "tired" of Nestes volatility and would rather have cash on hand, that's something I'd understand and applaud (and you should consider selling at that time).

So let's see what we have here.

Neste - the bottom was in, my estimate was correct.

In my previous articles, I've made it very clear that the primary correlation for Neste's results always was, and will continue to keep being, the margins and trends for its renewable fuels. During my early and later coverage, these have been negative - but as of the latest results, they have significantly improved. It's not wrong to call the turnaround a dramatic upswing in margin and pricing for renewable diesel. Of course, the reasons for outperformance was more than just that. The company's management has also implemented a turnaround plan, and while this is going to take time to see full effects, the results thus far have been encouraging.

My primary goal in this article is going to be to show you why I don't believe the current surge in valuation is more than the beginning for Neste, why we're bound to see much more upside than this.

First off, I want to point out how much, historically speaking, Neste has been able to outperform. The degree of "beating" that Neste has done over time is significant, because on a 2-year basis with a 20% margin of error, Neste has beaten estimates by more than that, 72%+ of the time. It's the highest "beating frequency" for a company this size thati have ever covered. Let me remind you at this time that Neste is neither small nor insignificant, but has a market capitalization of nearly €15.6B at the time of writing this article. It's also the largest refiner in all of Finland. So this forecast accuracy I am showing you here is not irrelevant.

The second argument or determination about upside and why more upside is more of a valuation-oriented and earnings-oriented one. In the case of either forecasted results or better than forecasted results, as I've said before and shown you above, the likelihood of outperformance here is significant. The company's results for 2025-2026E are likely to be between €0.5-€1/share. Even if the company doesn't beat those forecasts, the following years are currently showing even higher, with 2028E expectations putting it close to €2/share for that fiscal.

If that turns out to be accurate, even the current share price of over €20/share for the native is likely to be cheap for what Neste could offer.

Recent results confirm the company's overall upside. The current estimate is to deliver well in excess of the earlier estimated €300M EBITDA improvement by the end of 2026, improving fixed and variable costs while at the same time executing on profitable investments. These profitable investments include the Rotterdam capacity expansion, set to provide the company with yet more growth vectors.

Operational achievements should be the focus here; however, the quarterly results justify it. The company's SAF volumes, one of the more important indicators, were at record high levels, meaning that the company is leveraging the market that's currently offering very strong dynamics and potential for the business.

It's basically fair to say that the scenario that I have been forecasting on the positive side for the company has materialized, to some extent. It's not yet there in full - not entirely. That's where the upside for 2028E comes in.

The best explanation not only for Neste's long underperformance, but also for its recent upswing, is best done with an image/graphic. Remember how I said that renewable/SAF margins are the primary correlation - here is how that has looked, and how things look now.

As you can see, recovery is in full force here. Even the marketing & service segment saw improved trends and results, though this was as much due to seasonality as anything else. The company's investment activity has also gone down significantly, which is contributing to the upside and to the positive cash flow. Remember, though, that due to the company's specifics, cash flow will remain volatile here.

More importantly, perhaps, Neste has not significantly increased or exceeded its leverage or targets. For about 3 quarters now, the level has been at or close to 38%, which is below the target of 40%.

Regulatory news is neither small nor insignificant this latest period. The RED III directive, meaning it's a piece of legislation that EU members have to implement nationally over time, is going through transposition, including Central Europe. The ReFuelEU Aviation mandate is now at 2%, with further economic incentives in discussion. Imagine, for a second, an EU-wide implementation or requirement of environmental fuel for all aircraft fuelling or flying inside EU airspace. This is what we're talking about, and what I have been seeing in terms of upside for Neste for some time.

On the NA side of things, things are looking more mixed, which I believe is to be believed based on the current political administration.

On the one hand, the RVO, or renewable obligations, have increased for 2026-2027, but support only domestic feedstock and production. The LCFS amendments in California went into effect mid last year, which resulted in more balanced credit conditions, but most of the state ventures and programs with economic incentives support only domestic production. Because Neste's domestic NA production is comparatively low, it will make the EU market more important going forward, the NA market less so.

Generally speaking, I believe a continued bullish view on Neste, which I have, is dependent on continued high margins for renewable fuel and success in the company's investments and capacity expansions.

I believe the case for this is good, and therefore I am bullish.

Valuation for Neste - the risk is higher now, but the upside is still decent/high as well.

Neste has realized a lot of its upside that is implied in 2025 and 2026. What I mean by that is that if the company was to trade and generate as forecasted in the coming year, the current share price of €20 wouldn't be all that cheap in general - the upside instead comes from what happens in the years of 2027E and 2028E.

This is the main reason why this article represents a bit of a "risk increase" for Neste, and while taking profits its certainly not something I am doing just yet, is something that in certain situations could be entirely understandable at this price. The upside to a 20-year P/E average, which I believe to be a good forecast basis, is about 20% per year.

While this is certainly solid, it's not really anything earth-shattering. There are plenty of undervalued businesses in the market with that sort of upside, even with a better yield, and certainly less volatile than Neste. In order for you to invest here, you should believe in the fundamental appeal of renewable fuels and their expansion, because if that is the case, then this is potentially only the beginning for Neste. The suggested outperformance would also imply that there's much more upside here than analysts, investors, and the market may expect.

The obvious problem with such an assumption is the risk inherent in investing in it. How do we adjust or discount for this risk? I adjust for it by discounting the company's price to market. Instead of 18-19x P/E, I forecast, as I've mentioned before, at 15x. This implies a €28-€30/share fair value at most, which at this time would necessitate a PT increase to about €22.5/share, but not higher than this. That also represents my new price target and fair value range for the company, with the understanding that it might go higher (but also might go lower).

The energy sector is a tricky one. I'm more on the fence about it than I was 6 months ago, and am exiting some investments that I believe have reached their potential. Neste is, at least not just yet, not one of these investments, however.

For that reason, the risks and my thesis for the company are as follows

Risks to my thesis

Unfortunately, risks remain relatively prominent here, and are now supplemented with at least some valuation-related risks after more than a 100% RoR.

Neste is a play that's almost entirely dependent on volatile end-market refining and sales margins, which have proven themselves to be volatile in the past, and may do so again. While the recent moves, which are likely to make things more attractive here with minimum demands for SAF from the EU, are likely to somewhat improve things, a true recovery for Neste requires a recovery in end-market margins (which has happened as of 3Q, and is likely to continue, adding to this generally positive outlook)

Further risks include potential entry from established oil majors who are already committed to entering renewable markets, including BP (BP) and Chevron (CVX). The US domestic support could further add to this risk. I believe also that risks include continued trade policies, export trends, and the oil market and energy market overall.

I, however, believe that the risks are worth it—and at aa 22.5% upgrade to €22.5 PT on a fair value of €26-€30 for 2027-2028, I believe the upside is still worth it, although not nearly as much as previously. It makes my case for the company as an investment at lower multiples than now.

My updated PT for 2026 for the ADR NTOIF, which is a SHARE ADR, comes to a $26.14 price target, which I believe is good enough for investing.

My thesis for 2026 is now as follows.

Thesis

Neste is perhaps one of the most interesting oil/energy companies in Europe. They've found their niche, and they've pivoted at what I view as exactly the right time (proven by a significant return of over 100% since my last article) to serve a market that's going to need their products for the next few decades at the very least - and this continues to be my stance for 2026-2028E.

The recent surge and turnaround, I believe, coupled with the underlying trends in the energy industry and institutional support for renewable fuels, continues to make a case for investing in the company.

At a current forward valuation of under 15x, based on an estimated fair value above €26/share and a PT of €22.5/share updated for the 2026E period as of January, I consider Neste to be a "BUY". It's not a speculative one, but it's not as stable as some. The company is cheap, under €10/share, which is the cost basis for about 45% of my position in the company I hold right now.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of €22.5/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a “Buy rating as of January 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Hims & Hers And HIMZ: Short‑Term Pain From Novo, Long‑Term Opportunity Still Bright
data.description:
Intro

In my previous analysis of Hims & Hers Health, Inc. (HIMS) back in October, I focused heavily on the company’s founding story, its founders, and the long‑term growth prospects management has outlined — including their ambition to reach $3 billion in adjusted EBITDA by 2030. In my view, when evaluating a company that is disrupting an industry and building an innovative new business model, it’s far more important to assess the leadership team, their vision, and the long‑term financial targets they put forward. The real question is whether the founders have the ability to execute on those goals — which are often intentionally ambitious in the world of startups and other disruptors — rather than fixating on short-term financial results.

I think many would agree that Tesla (TSLA) would never have become what it is today without Elon Musk — yet a decade ago, analysts were obsessing over whether the company beat or missed earnings by a penny or two, or if gross margins slipped a few percentage points here and there. Back then, Tesla's market cap of around $30 billion was only a fraction of today's $1.5 trillions, and having held positions at the time, selling away way too early, I remember clearly that the market started to revalue Tesla’s stock far ahead of any true strengthening in their financials.

What I’m trying to say with this is that, in my view, HIMS’s value is driven more by narrative and belief than by the numbers. And for that narrative to play out, the management team needs to be capable of executing on these ambitious goals. The fact that HIMS is already profitable and generating free cash flow at what I think it’s fair to say a relatively early stage of its journey is remarkable in itself. So far, I think we can say that the company’s leader and co‑founder, Andrew Dudum, has delivered on what he’s promised, and in my view, there’s no reason to believe he can’t continue on the same successful path despite the inevitable short‑term bumps along the way.

A few notable dynamics that have materialized since my last analysis

At the time, I rated HIMS as a Hold — not because I questioned the company’s vision or business model, but because the share price felt too demanding, trading around $50 back then. I wrote:

While I like their long-term vision, the reasoning behind this stance is the high degree of near-term uncertainties.

Additionally, I pointed out:

If the price of the FDA-approved drugs is successfully negotiated down to $150 per month, it will certainly be a huge headwind to the HIMS...

That turned out to be the right assumption, as I can judge today — a lot has happened since then, and prices of both Novo Nordisk (NVO) and Eli Lilly (LLY) weight-loss drugs have been negotiated to really affordable levels.

The very latest Novo announcement, which did not include HIMS on the list of partners selling the new, red-hot Wegovy pill, despite previous headlines about discussions, was the latest blow, pushing the stock even lower. On top of that, Amazon (AMZN) steps in as well, offering Wegovy pills starting as low as $25 (co-pay for a prescribed medication).

I flagged that risk back in October, but I downplayed it at the time — a judgment that, in hindsight, proved to be a mistake.

The second most important risk remains Amazon Pharmacy, a threat known since Hims' inception. However, I believe this risk may be somewhat mitigated at the moment. Amazon has no clear pathway to compete directly with Novo and Lilly's direct-to-consumer offerings, and the legal risks associated with Hims' style of compounded drugs are, in my opinion, unacceptable for Amazon. I believe Amazon will avoid this market until there is much better visibility.

Novo successfully brought Amazon on board, while HIMS was left out of the partners list.

From the $50s in October to around $30 today — quite a ride for HIMS stock, but the reasons behind it are clear, as the weight-loss segment has seen as a main growth driver for the company.

The overwhelming volatility is why I decided to highlight Defiance Daily Target 2X Long HIMS ETF (HIMZ) in this article – a vehicle based on HIMS daily movements but leveraged 2x. In my view, it’s better for investors to avoid this instrument, as it’s inherently speculative and structured in a way that produces unfavorable long-term outcomes. However, it can still be a useful tool for trading-oriented individuals looking to capitalize on the strong intraday movements that are periodically quite common with HIMS. I’ll revisit this topic after reviewing HIMS’ recent developments.

Recent developments

Earnings

Hims’ latest earnings were slightly better than expected. Adjusted EPS came in at $0.28, beating estimates by $0.05, while revenue reached $599 million, topping expectations by $19 million. The company narrowed its full‑year 2025 adjusted EBITDA outlook to $307M–$317M, down from the prior $295M–$335M range. It also tightened its revenue guidance to $2.335B–$2.355B, compared with $2.3B–$2.4B previously, while analyst consensus sat at $2.34B.

Novo left Hims out in the cold again

Somewhat oddly, in my view, as such things are usually not formally announced, HIMS revealed alongside its earnings release that it is once again in ongoing partnership talks with Novo Nordisk:

Hims & Hers and Novo Nordisk are in active discussions to make Wegovy injections and Novo Nordisk’s oral Wegovy (when FDA approved) available through the Hims & Hers platform, advancing consumer options and pursuant to prescriber recommendation. Hims & Hers notes that discussions are ongoing, no definitive agreement has been executed with Novo Nordisk, and there is a possibility that no definitive agreement may ever be executed with Novo Nordisk.

Citi analyst noted after Novo’s last week announcement of Wegovy pill availability and partnerships, summarized by MSN:

Citi analyst believes Novo Nordisk’s (NVO) announcement this morning that its Wegovy weight loss pill is now broadly available throughout the U.S. will pressure Hims & Hers (HIMS). Hims was again left off Novo’s partners list, which includes CVS Health (CVS) and Costco (COST), LifeMD (LFMD) and GoodRx (GDRX), which likely reflects disagreements between Novo and Hims around Him’s compounded semaglutide strategy, the analyst tells investors in a research note. Citi thinks Novo’s pill launch will put pressure on Hims & Hers’ GLP-1 business in 2026. The firm keeps a Sell rating on the shares with a $30 price target.

I fully agree with that conclusion — Novo can’t accept HIMS compounding semaglutide while simultaneously giving them a partnership to sell oral Wegovy. On the other hand, semaglutide compounding is currently one of the key pillars in HIMS’s growth narrative, so I genuinely wondered how a partnership could work while allowing HIMS to continue compounding at the same time. In the end, it turned out it couldn’t.

Labs went live in November

Although the partnership with Novo to sell oral Wegovy hasn’t moved forward as planned, another initiative mentioned during the last earnings call has been successfully launched, according to the earnings release:

We plan to launch comprehensive whole body lab testing before year-end, which will represent a significant step forward in this evolution. By scaling capabilities like blood testing, we're thrilled to help more customers affordably access the insights they need to monitor, maintain and improve their overall health. We envision these capabilities empowering consumers to proactively address health concerns such as vitamin deficiencies, unoptimized hormonal levels and genetic risk factors while also identifying early indicators of more serious conditions like cancer and cardiovascular disease.

Hims made an announcement on Nov 13: Introducing Labs by Hims & Hers.

As it’s a very important part of their growth story, in my view, moving forward, I’ve put together a short summary of how it really works:

The process begins in the app, where users choose a testing plan and, if eligible, schedule a blood draw at a nearby clinic. Once results are ready, the app delivers biomarker data, category‑level analysis, and a doctor‑developed action plan focused on lifestyle guidance. Depending on clinical judgment, users may also gain access to personalized treatment plans, with consultations completed directly through the app.

I think that it’s pretty obvious that after selling a customer such a test, very few will return without at least some minor issues. There’s a great chance that most people might need at least some kind of vitamin, as vitamin deficiencies can show up in test results. That’s why I think testing is so important—it not only helps show the client what they need to stay or get healthy, but it’s also a great chance to build a closer, stronger, and longer-term relationship with the patient or customer, whichever term you prefer.

Preventive care appears to be an important part of their overall strategy, as the investment in Grail was further discussed during the earnings call:

..we made a strategic investment in GRAIL, the company behind what we believe is the most advanced multi-cancer early detection test on the market.

...and HIMS CEO offered a more generous perspective on testing and preventative care:

These recent developments are an exciting glimpse into a future where access to proactive preventative care is not a privilege but a standard

Expanding to the UK and Canada

Another key aspect of the expansion strategy, boldly emphasized during the earnings call, has also seen progress:

Looking ahead, we see international markets as a powerful long-term growth opportunity, representing more than $1 billion in potential annual revenue.

We believe Hims & Hers will soon become the largest global consumer health platform, unlocking access to direct-to-consumer personalized care for more customers than any company in the world.

Canada went live at the beginning of December, and the UK offerings expanded to include a weight-loss program that same month.

Long-term outlook confirmed

The CFO made a statement during the earnings call:

With the immense opportunity in front of us, our conviction remains high in our ability to meet or exceed the 2030 targets we established earlier this year, at least $6.5 billion in revenue and $1.3 billion in adjusted EBITDA.

Valuation

If we look at analysts’ estimates through 2030, it’s clear they don’t share management’s bold vision — analysts project much lower revenue, averaging around $4.18 billion by 2030, versus HIMS’s own target of $6.5 billion. I agree that a company like HIMS needs ambitious goals, and analysts, for their part, build assumptions based on their models, which don’t necessarily have to align with management’s outlook. With so many moving parts to consider going forward, I think investors ultimately have to decide for themselves whether they believe management can deliver on those ambitious targets.

Based on analysts’ implied 2030 P/E of around 16 — which may even be conservative given their lower growth assumptions compared to what HIMS has outlined — I think the stock is, from my perspective, trading roughly around justified value at the moment.

Risks

In my view, the primary risk is concentrated in the weight‑loss drug segment. Right now, the new pricing initiatives from leaders Lilly and Novo, combined with HIMS being left off Novo’s partner list, put the company’s short‑term outlook under meaningful pressure. The lawsuits related to compounding also represent a significant source of risk.

I also believe that founder and CEO Andrew Dudum’s role as the key visionary is crucial, which means execution risk remains elevated given how much depends on a single visionary.

HIMZ

As I mentioned earlier, I’m also taking a brief look at the ETF based on HIMS.

The fund summary is as follows:

Tidal Trust II – Defiance Daily Target 2X Long HIMS ETF is a U.S.-domiciled, actively managed fund launched in March 2025. It seeks to deliver 2× the daily performance of Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) by using derivatives such as swaps and listed options. The ETF is non‑diversified, fully concentrated in HIMS’s daily price movement, and carries an expense ratio of 1.29%.

From the funds prospect:

Investment Objective :

The Fund seeks daily investment results, before fees and expenses, of two times (200%) the daily percentage change in the share price of Hims & Hers Health Inc. (NYSE: HIMS). The Fund does not seek to achieve its stated investment objective for a period other than a single trading day.

It’s generally not suited for holding overnight. A great illustration of this is the comparison between HIMS and HIMZ, where it is seen how the compounding effect causes the ETF to diverge from the underlying stock:

The green line represents HIMS, while the candlesticks indicate HIMZ. The chart is from the launch in March 2025.

As mentioned earlier, I think it’s better to avoid investing through this vehicle, but it’s still useful to recognize it as a tool for traders who want intraday leveraged exposure. HIMS can be highly volatile at times and often trending strongly, making this ETF relevant for short‑term tactical trading.

Please review the risks of leveraged ETFs as explained in the linked SEC bulletin before placing any trades in this ETF. These risks include extreme volatility, time and volatility decay, and the real possibility of losing all of your investment. The normal risks can be exaggerated with single-stock ETFs such as this. Only experienced traders who fully understand the risks should trade them.

Takeaway

I like the HIMS narrative a great deal, but the current short-term uncertainties justify maintaining a Hold rating for now. Had the company secured a path to offering an oral version of Wegovy, I would have leaned toward a Buy. Even so, I believe we are approaching a point where the risk–reward profile becomes very compelling.

I believe Andrew Dudum and his team have the ability to successfully navigating current challenges, and their long-term vision remains really compelling. The blood-testing-driven approach is particularly promising, and I think they are on a strong trajectory with that initiative. I’ll be monitoring HIMS closely, ready to upgrade my rating as visibility improves.

data.title: Ellington Credit: Chasing High Carry In CLO Equity And Mezzanine Tranches (Hold)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Ellington Credit Company (EARN) is a closed-end fund that, by investing in the riskiest tranche of CLOs ( account the situation of credit spreads and a price above NAV that's no longer at a discount, our view is to hold or wait for better conditions to enter.

CEF Overview

Until recently, EARN was Ellington Residential Mortgage operating as a REIT and had a completely different business model from the current one, as its portfolio was primarily mortgage-focused, invested mainly in Agency RMBS.

As of April 1, 2025, the transition took effect, and the company became a closed-end fund, focusing its strategy on corporate credit. That said, in this article, when analyzing the vehicle, we will only take into account data from that moment onwards.

As a closed-end fund, Ellington Credit does not seek to track an index and is structured as a RIC. It's important to understand that in ETFs, APs keep the price very close to the NAV through the creation/redemption mechanism. Unlike ETFs, closed-end funds do not rely on a creation/redemption mechanism to keep market price aligned with NAV. As a result, shares can trade at persistent premiums or discounts to NAV, making the decision to choose the timing of entry more important.

Despite maintaining a constant distribution of $0.08/share per month throughout 2025, EARN traded at a discount with notable episodes early in the year and again in September/October.

In December, there was a sharp acceleration in the trend, breaking above the 0% (PAR) level, which had capped the shares mid-year. Given that the dividend has not changed, this adjustment is driven primarily by share-price appreciation (as shown in the following graph).

After that peak and following a correction, the trend toward the premium zone has continued until it has once again crossed the 0% zone, this time with greater momentum. In my view, this trend is partly caused by recent interest rate cuts and a movement/rotation of capital and increased demand for higher-yielding investments. Furthermore, if we add to this the fact that the US economy is growing (plus easier credit conditions), inflation remains contained, and employment is stable, this movement and the new trend that's forming lead me to suspect that, at least during the first half of 2026, opportunities to enter at a discount will be scarce or sporadic and that the premium will be the prevailing regime. Therefore, from a discount/premium-to-NAV standpoint, it would be prudent to wait for a discount window or maintain the position.

In its Form N-2, it states that under normal conditions at least 80% of the NAV will be invested in credit and credit-related instruments. Similarly, it states that this policy may be modified by the board provided that 60 days' notice is given to shareholders. In terms of implementation and risk control, the prospectus stipulates that the use of derivatives is permitted and that leverage may also be employed through the issuance of senior securities, which amplifies both gains and losses in the NAV and adds risks of rollover, margin calls, and the possibility of being forced into deleveraging.

Also, the document itself shows us in detail CLO tranche exposure that the CEF is currently focusing on, primarily in mezzanine and equity.

In other words, as an investor, it’s important to understand the CEF is not a defensive carry or a cash sleeve, and the document states that it's not risk-free: Leveraged loan risk, collateralized loan obligation (CLO) risk, valuation risk, liquidity risk, etc. All of these are broken down and explained in the Risk Factors section, but for me, the most relevant ones in this type of instrument are valuation and liquidity because they're “easy” to monitor through macro factors such as tightening of financial conditions, rate hikes, widening spreads, etc. These are environments in which investors demand a higher risk premium, which tends to pressure valuations while increasing expected credit stress across the underlying loan collateral.

Likewise, in the latest closing documentation shared by the fund, EARN confirms everything we have described about its operations. In the portfolio overview, we can see that 96.4% of the portfolio is made up of CLOs, of which 50% correspond to mezzanine tranches and 50% to equity.

Macro Factors

Given that EARN invests in the riskiest tranche of the CLO, we can review the High Yield OAS (BAMLH0A0HYM2) as a benchmark for measuring current credit risk. The chart shows that the compensation investors require to hold high-yield credit is near five-year lows. Except for the rebound in March-April, which was a consequence of the uncertainty and fear that shook the markets following the announcement of tariffs (which ultimately did not shake the economy as many predicted), therefore, rather than something structural, it's an “anomalous” deviation, with the HY OAS range settling into a sideways range with minimal fluctuations of 0.5% between 3% and 2.5%. Despite tariffs and fears of an inflationary spike, uncertainty surrounding the Fed's messages throughout the year (except for the latest statements on Dec. 10), the narrative of the AI bubble, etc., what has dominated during the year is a regime of confidence and benign credit.

Furthermore, if we look at the employment data, there are no warning signs indicating a recession. Initial jobless claims remain near cycle lows, not exceeding the range of recent years and showing no upward trend, which would be a negative sign to take into account, as it would indicate that something is not right in the economy when more and more people are claiming benefits every week. However, this is not the case, and until it is, I see no reason to change my view.

In addition to the initial claims data, the US Services PMI rebounded in December, indicating that the country's economic activity shows no signs of slowing down. This shows no signs of recession and may even be re-accelerating if the trend continues, although the report itself currently points out that a price index that's still falling but remains high and a backlog of orders that is falling reflect a situation of resilient growth rather than a linear re-acceleration.

Conclusion

EARN invests in the highest-risk tranches of CLO structures, delivering a very attractive distribution yield but one that is not without risk and is much more sensitive to changes in macroeconomic conditions (financial conditions, interest rates, economic growth, etc.). This makes it a recurring income vehicle rather than a cash substitute type with an attractive forward distribution yield of 17.55%.

Similarly, the entry point is important, and it's best to do so when it's trading at a discount to NAV, especially since spreads, even with no signs of widening, are compressed to minimums, and any negative news or factors will have a significant impact.

All in all, if you already have a position, I would recommend HOLD, keeping an eye on credit conditions and accompanying macro factors. Likewise, if you were thinking of entering, I would not recommend doing so at the present time and would wait for a discount window to open while the credit regime remains favorable and spreads remain within range.

data.title: The Gold/Silver Ratio Is Signaling 'Wartime Behavior'
data.description:
Since Jerome Powell signaled the end of the tightening cycle in October of 2023, we've talked about the long-awaited narrowing of the performance gap between mega-cap tech and the rest of the market (particularly small caps).

It hasn't happened. It's been a series of head fakes.

Why?

Because the Fed has been tapping the brakes all along (through real rates and messaging), countering an economy with fiscal and industrial policy tailwinds.

But as we entered 2026, we're finally seeing monetary and fiscal policy moving in the same direction. And as we discussed last week, the "diffusion" (the spread/distribution) of AI and the productivity gains are beginning to show up in the data. That's a setup for capital to spread across the market.

And we're seeing it in the new year. Small caps are finally outperforming.

Now, here’s the other chart we’ve been watching - the one that tends to signal regime change...

Over time in these notes, we’ve tracked this gold/silver ratio as a signal of extreme moments of safe haven demand.

Importantly, in the chart, the extreme peaks line up with big events (WW2, the Gulf War, the pandemic).

And the sequence is consistent: Gold leads as global capital flows to the relative safe haven asset. Silver later follows (higher) partly as a cheaper "safe haven," but also because it's an industrial metal.

And in the prior peaks, as we've discussed along the way, it's the outsized rise in silver that pushes the ratio back down.

That's exactly what we've seen. Since we revisited this ratio in the fall, silver has ripped, and the ratio has now “puked” lower from an extreme reading.

So what is it communicating?

Historically, this represents a new phase where fear of war turns into wartime behavior: military buildup, reshoring, supply chain hardening.

That's happening.

On that note, let's talk about the National Security Strategy document the White House posted in November (you can see it here).

This document explains many of the events that have taken place in the first couple of weeks of 2026, including this about the Western Hemisphere: "we will assert and enforce a 'Trump Corollary' to the Monroe Doctrine."

And there are two other key assertions in what Trump called this "roadmap."

First, on China, the document says deterring a conflict over Taiwan is priority, mostly because of the threat to global shipping through the South China Sea. It says it requires a vigilant posture, a renewed defense industrial base, greater military investment, and winning the economic and technological competition.

And it's blunt on Europe. It calls out the European Union and "other transnational bodies" for undermining political liberty and sovereignty through migration policies, censorship, and loss of national identities and self-confidence. It explicitly questions whether or not they will be strong enough "to remain reliable allies."

Expect this to create some fireworks next week when Trump gives a speech at the World Economic Forum (to a room full of the parties he is calling out).

Original Post

data.title: ACWI Provides Global Large-Cap Equity Exposure
data.description:
The iShares MSCI ACWI ETF (ACWI) is a passively managed international equity exchange-traded fund designed to track the performance of domestic & international developed and emerging market companies. The strategy can be best utilized by investors seeking equity exposure to foreign markets without losing domestic growth in a low-cost ETF wrapper. Those that are seeking to invest in the ACWI Index ex-US can consider the iShares MSCI ACWI ex-US ETF (ACWX).

About iShares MSCI ACWI ETF

ACWI was launched on March 26, 2008 by iShares on the Nasdaq Exchange. The strategy has a low expense ratio of 32bps, though funds offered by Schwab, Vanguard, and State Street [SPDR] may offer international exposure for a much lower cost.

ACWI is a highly liquid strategy with over $26 billion in net assets with an average of $517 million in share value changing hands on a daily basis. This means that the ETF can be easily traded on the open market with minimal spread risk.

ACWI pays out a modest semiannual distribution with an annualized rate of $2.20/share, yielding 1.52%. Distributions have exhibited modest growth over the years, providing investors with the addition of a small income component on top of expected capital returns.

ACWI was designed to track the MSCI ACWI Index, which was designed to track the performance of developed and emerging markets, inclusive of US equities. The Index uses a free float-adjusted market capitalization weighting methodology, providing exposure across large- and mid-cap companies. The Index provides exposure across 23 developed markets and 24 emerging market countries, reflecting the performance of 2,517 constituents. The average Index constituent market capitalization is $36.99 billion.

Given that the Index is market capitalization-weighted, US equities may play a major role in portfolio performance. Accordingly, US-based constituents make up 64% of the total weight of the Index. In addition to this, the information technology sector holds the largest sector weight at 27.22% given the sheer size of US technology companies, particularly the Magnificent 7.

Despite the weighting factors, the top 10 holdings account for 24.3% of the total portfolio weight. Accordingly, the Magnificent 7 are the top 7 stocks within ACWI and make up 21.58% of the total portfolio weight at the time of writing. By comparison, the Magnificent 7 make up 34.90% of the S&P 500 Index weight, as exemplified by the . Net of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM), the vast majority of the top holdings are domiciled within the US; of the top 100 holdings, 68% are US-based.

Investor Suitability

ACWI can be utilized as a core investment as the baseline for a diversified investment strategy. Essentially, ACWI can be utilized as the foundation for constructing an investor’s portfolio, providing broad diversification across US and international equities. From there, investors can add satellite positions to enhance concentration across specific sectors, regions, themes, or other types of funds or individual equities.

Though investing in ACWI will provide investors with substantial exposure to US-based stocks, the strategy may be appealing for investors that aim to gain diversified exposure without the need to differentiate between individual international or emerging market ETFs. Given the roughly 60/40 allocation to US and ex-US equities, ACWI may be an appropriate allocation for international equity exposure.

From a broader viewpoint, investors may want to also consider utilizing closed-end funds [CEFs] for a more dynamic investment approach to gaining international equity exposure. Investing in international equities can be a daunting task given the diversification across sector and region, creating differentiated markets that are oftentimes aggregated into single investment strategies. A challenge with indexed ETF strategies is that they may not capture alpha across these factors, potentially limiting an investor’s full upside potential to market beta. Investors seeking a more dynamic approach may want to consider closed-end funds managed by Eaton Vance, which was acquired by Morgan Stanley (MS), abrdn plc, BlackRock, Inc. (BLK), and Calamos Investments, amongst others. That being said, investing in managed strategies exposes the investor to manager risk, which entails the risk of allocating to the wrong sectors and regions at the wrong time.

Risks Related to ACWI

ACWI provides investors with broad diversification across developed and emerging markets, potentially exposing investors to certain risks that should be considered prior to making a final investment decision. Given the broad diversification of the strategy, ACWI may primarily be limited to beta risk, limiting the upside potential achieved from a dynamic international investment strategy. Investors may be exposed to geopolitical risks as well as macroeconomic risks like inflation and interest rate policy, amongst other factors.

Final Thoughts

ACWI provides investors with a broad, diversified international equity strategy with exposure to US and international developed and emerging markets. The strategy may be best utilized as a core equity holding for a diversified portfolio strategy.

This article answers three main questions about ACWI:

  1. What characteristics do ACWI's holdings have?
  2. How does ACWI reflect its underlying index?
  3. How can ACWI fit in a portfolio?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Rio Tinto: Up A Lot, Still A Good Outlook
data.description:
Article Thesis

Rio Tinto Group (RIO) is one of the largest mining companies in the world and benefits from growing demand for a range of commodities. Shares have performed well over the last year but aren't expensive, and profits are forecasted to grow nicely this year. For investors seeking a nice dividend yield and exposure to global commodity and base materials markets, Rio Tinto remains a compelling choice.

Past Coverage

I have written about Rio Tinto Group here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently in August 2024. I gave the company a Buy rating back then, arguing that shares traded at a too-low valuation, while Rio Tinto also benefited from several macro trends. This call worked out well so far, as Rio Tinto has returned a little more than 40% since that article was published, versus a return of a little more than 20% from the broad market over the same time frame. With more than a year having passed since my last update on Rio Tinto, and with several news items that are of importance for the company coming out in January, it is time for me to update my views on the company today.

Rio Tinto: Relevant News Items

The resource industries generally are capital intense, and bringing on new production can easily cost billions, while greenfield projects also can take many years from the decision to pursue them until they start producing. It is thus not surprising that growing via acquisitions and mergers is an often-used approach for players in these industries. Rio Tinto is a company that has engaged in M&A repeatedly in the past, and we recently got the news that RIO is talking about a deal with Glencore (GLCNF, GLNCY).

There is, for now, no guarantee that a deal will happen, and we also do not yet know what such a deal would look like -- a full-on merger is a possibility, but it's also possible that RIO will only acquire some of the assets currently owned by Glencore. If a deal happens, that could be quite positive for Rio Tinto -- depending on the valuation at which it happens, and depending on what synergies Rio Tinto would be able to capture over time.

Rio Tinto has a pretty clean balance sheet, with a net debt position of $14 billion -- around half of this year's expected EBITDA -- so it would not be hard for Rio Tinto to finance a deal, although an all-out merger with Glencore would likely require a stock-for-stock component. It makes sense for investors to watch for potential deal announcements in the coming months, as a well-crafted deal could be a driver for Rio Tinto and its shares in the future.

Rio Tinto also recently announced a deal with Amazon.com (AMZN) that will see RIO supply the latter with especially clean/ environmentally friendly copper. Amazon, as one of the largest data center operators in the world that keeps investing many billions of dollars in new data centers every year, needs large amounts of copper. With this deal, Amazon.com will be the first customer of RIO's "clean copper" that is produced by its Nuton subsidiary. While this deal alone will, I believe, not have a huge impact on RIO's fundamentals yet, I think that this deal shows that demand for cleaner/greener commodities exists, so this business could grow considerably over time when RIO starts selling clean copper to other companies, too.

We also got some politics news that could be of relevance for Rio Tinto. A group of lawmakers across both parties introduced a bill that would see the U.S. stockpile a range of critical commodities. No matter whether that bill ultimately gets passed, I think this shows that politicians across many countries are increasingly seeing the importance of reliable commodity supply, having reserves, being able to supply the country's needs from secure sources, and so on. Overall, this should result in more support for mining companies like Rio Tinto, I believe, and could help them with future project approvals, regulation, and so on -- many countries, including the U.S., want to become less dependent on at-risk suppliers.

Rio Tinto: Performance And Outlook

This week, Rio Tinto also announced how it performed during the most recent quarter when it comes to growing its production volumes. The company was able to hit a new record level of production during the most recent quarter, beating a record that had been in place for almost a decade. Almost 91 million tons, across different commodities, were delivered in the December quarter. Annualized, that's more than 360 million tons -- well above the result for the entire year, which was around 330 million tons. This shows that there was some acceleration throughout the last year, which, of course, is positive. This was made possible by an especially strong iron ore performance from Rio Tinto, as the company benefited from healthy demand in China -- with recently improving iron ore pricing also indicating that demand is far from bad:

In the above chart, we see that iron ore (SCO:COM) prices firmed noticeably over the last half year, and this trend may remain in place as 2026 is forecasted to be a good year for economic growth across the U.S., India, China, and so on.

It is thus, I believe, not surprising to see that analysts are forecasting that Rio Tinto will see appealing earnings growth this year: Relative to the forecasted earnings per share of $6.55 in 2025, 2026's earnings per share are forecasted to grow by around 12% to a little north of $7.30. While far from Nvidia-like (NVDA) growth, a double-digit growth rate is quite compelling, especially when combined with a nice dividend yield and a valuation that isn't overly high -- and that's the case with Rio Tinto.

Rio Tinto: Yield And Valuation

At current prices, Rio Tinto offers a dividend yield of around 4.5%, which is pretty good. That being said, RIO has a history of adjusting its dividend to its profitability level over time -- this has resulted in dividend reductions at times, so the dividend could easily decline should earnings pull back. Since profits are forecasted to grow this year, however, I do think that the near-term outlook for the dividend is good -- but investors should expect a (temporary) dividend reduction in case commodity markets were to take a hit.

Based on the forecasted earnings per share for 2026, Rio Tinto currently trades at an earnings multiple of around 12 -- not the lowest valuation it has traded at, but also far from a high valuation. Looking at Rio Tinto's enterprise value to EBITDA multiple, we see the following:

Rio Tinto trades marginally below the longer-term average, and a 5x to 6x EBITDA multiple also seems far from expensive in absolute terms. This underlines my belief that Rio Tinto is a very solid value, although not a mega-bargain, at current prices.

Takeaway

Should the world enter a recession, commodity demand would take a hit, and so would RIO's profitability, and the dividend would likely get reduced in such a scenario. But I think that a recession isn't very likely in the near term -- for now, the outlook for Rio Tinto is good.

Strong iron ore production levels and firming prices should be good for Rio Tinto in 2026, and M&A, "green commodity" ventures, etc. provide growth potential, too. For someone looking for commodity exposure, Rio Tinto looks attractive, I believe.
